subject: Chemistry
title: Road Salt: Melting Ice with Chemistry
description: A short lesson to help you apply Road Salt: Melting Ice with Chemistry.

hook.text: Spreading salt on icy roads lowers the freezing point of water, turning ice back into liquid.

buildup.text: Pure water freezes at 0°C. Dissolve sodium chloride in it and the freezing point drops to about -21°C.

discovery.text: Salt ions disrupt the crystal structure of ice. Water molecules can't organize into a solid as easily.

twist.text: Below -21°C, salt stops working. That's why northern countries use calcium chloride, which works down to -32°C.

climax.text: Road salt corrodes cars and poisons waterways. It costs billions in environmental damage annually.

punchline.text: Salt disrupts ice crystals. Simple chemistry that saves—and damages—roads.

quiz.question: How does road salt melt ice?
options: It lowers water's freezing point by disrupting ice crystal formation
It generates heat that melts the ice
It absorbs moisture from the air to dissolve ice
